summary: Gruber revisits the ongoing MacBook Wi-Fi security exploit controversy involving researchers David Maynor and Jon Ellch, who demonstrated a purported wireless hijacking at Black Hat in August 2006. More than six months later, Maynor broke his silence but only demonstrated a denial-of-service crash against Mac OS X 10.4.6, not the full remote code execution hijack shown in the original video. Gruber methodically catalogs the still-unanswered questions: the unnamed third-party Wi-Fi card, the suspicious MAC address evidence, and why the exploit targets an OS version predating the original announcement. He argues that a kernel panic is far removed from the claimed machine hijacking and that Apple's boilerplate 'may cause arbitrary code execution' language proves nothing. Gruber concludes that while Maynor likely did discover a legitimate DoS vulnerability, there remains no evidence it can be expanded into a reliable hijack exploit.
key_points: Maynor's new demonstration only showed a kernel panic (DoS) on Mac OS X 10.4.6, not the root-privilege hijacking from the original Black Hat video
Jim Thompson's analysis of the video revealed the exploited network interface had an Apple MAC address, suggesting the unnamed third-party USB card may have been a distraction
The exploit was demonstrated against 10.4.6 despite 10.4.7 being released a month before the original announcement, raising questions about whether Apple's September patches were even related
Apple's 'may cause arbitrary code execution' language is boilerplate used for virtually all buffer overflow advisories and doesn't confirm a working hijack exists
Maynor's former employer SecureWorks apparently prevented him from presenting evidence to defend his claims, muddying the entire saga
money_quotes: Most of the time, when I report about something, the job is organizing, ordering, and explaining the facts. With the Maynor/Ellch/SecureWorks MacBook Wi-Fi security saga, however, it's always been about missing facts.
Many people remain under the impression that the controversy is about whether they used a third-party card or not, a fact which was never actually in dispute.

There is a real controversy that is converse of the false one — that their video purports to show an exploit against some still-unnamed third-party card but in fact shows a MacBook that is connected to the network using its built-in AirPort card.
That's bad, but it's far removed from the 'take over the machine' hijacking shown in their video.
Apple uses this language for just about any buffer overflow bug, however.
But I don't think this exploit can necessarily be expanded into a reliable hijack. We'll know more if and when Maynor releases code for his exploit, which he's promised to do.
key_insight: A demonstrated kernel panic is a legitimate but far lesser finding than the sensational machine-hijacking claim, and without released exploit code or reproducible evidence, extraordinary security claims remain unproven.
